item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image is owned by Gregory Cope and is licensed under All Rights Reserved. As such, it is not compliant with CC 3.0, and has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm kind of confused why the initial broadcast tells the public to seek fallout shelters, then immediately after tells them not to leave their homes. Is the announcer being deliberately confusing, even at that early stage?

Otherwise, I like this enough to give it a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kind of expected it to use the code to name you if you were signed in at the end :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Super-late post to say the changes to this were worthwhile. I was admittedly rolling my eyes at yet another skip that knows Foundation stuff, but then it calls out its own number in the sequence and apparently goes insane, and I just love that for some reason. :)

Well, that quickly went from "weird but interesting" to "creepy as hell."

Have upvote #50, you crazy TV.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. The contradicting statements, the info breach about the Foundation, the changing tone and the fact that this could actually spread false information (and possible lure the viewers to it) , whatever it might do. The numbers are called, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be honest, I liked it better when it was actually radioactive. Considering that the other world is probably a nuked wasteland at this point, it feels appropriate for a window into that world to leak.

Right now, it's blander than the first draft. I'd suggest playing up the radioactive aspect more, not less.

For example, how about linking lucidity of the speaker, date of events, and radio together? It could range from 0,2 Sv (give you a higher cancer risk, when it's generic emergency announcements) to 3 Sv (hairloss, death probable within 30 days, when it calls the numbers) to 20 Sv (lethal within 3-7 days, when it's lucid enough to be capable of communicating with the Foundation). It might add if the announcer was suffering from radiation sickness appropriate for the radiation, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wikidot doesn't update karma live, but only once every week(?) or so, maybe longer. So at some point between the last update and now, you did enough to bump up, but it wasn't reflected until now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, my karmas suddenly gone from 0 to high.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My interpretation is a presentation like a classic news report.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No.

I'm currently looking for a good still frame

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One question: Is channel 0 just static with an announcer speaking? Or do you actually see an announcer?

Up-voted, I like :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the new edits; I'd still like a description of the announcer though.

The "I see you" bits remind me of the final section of SCP-1981. Is that deliberate?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm keeping my downvote. There isn't much to this beyond and it being a broadcast station, and there's already an SCP that does that better. While I understand the focus of this particular broadcast station is that it's breaching containment, that's part of my problem with this SCP. It doesn't say anything about when it wasn't contained. Was it spouting it's SCP number before we brought it in? Was it always breaching containment?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Majorly edited

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I might rewrite it so it's anomalous properties are much more focused on the apocalyptic scenario and drop the radioactivity, but no, I'm not going to make it about multiple end of the world scenarios. Some of the appeal is the fact that, first, it is a Foundation's apocalypse, one directly caused by on-site nuclear warheads, and second, it's self-awareness and threat of containment breach.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted. But I'm seconding the concerns raised by many others about it being unnecessarily deadly. The addendum was enough to tip me into liking it, however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I'd be much more interested if:

A) The radiation effect was dropped.

B) Instead of there being only one PSA on a loop, if every PSA played described a different end-of-the-world scenario as if it were happening. The addendum would be kept, but instead of being the only script, it would be a transcript of an incident where the item demonstrated knowledge of the SCP Foundation and self-awareness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, has anyone a good still frame for this? I can't find what I'm looking for, an old-timey black and white colour news announcer kind of guy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm currently writing up a few other logs. Just give me time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's pretty obvious to me that it's some kind of window into an alternate universe in which everything went wrong and the Foundation nuked everything.

It gives off radiation because everything's nuked.

It kills you not due to any property of the object, but because, you know, radiation is harmful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the addendum, the "I have breached containment" thing. The whole radiation deal feels tacked on, though. That's not what's interesting here.

What might make it work even better would be if it wasn't a loop, or if the loop changed occasionally. Otherwise, well, why broadcast a loop? That undermines the whole "it only gets one channel" part. It could just as easily be on videotape.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an interesting article…But as Reject said, there is something about this that just annoys the hell out of me, and I can't tell what.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This may not be top rank SCP material, but it is fairly interesting. The only thing I really have a problem with is that it randomly just gives off radiation that kills you for no real reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really get the "meh" reception. I really enjoyed this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think Gears's Razor is more applicable here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's something about this that irks me immensely. I can't place my finger on what it is though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not a fan of the tacked-on rads, but still interesting enough for a tentative +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, I didn't see the radiation poisoning part when I read it the first time. I'd take that out entirely, because, come on :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The broadcast is kind of cool, but I don't like the tacked-on radiation effect, or the idea that someone's old TV knows about the Foundation.

This would have been brilliant as part of Game Day, if one of the Foundation's internal security monitors started doing this during the containment breach. As a separate SCP, I'm not so sure about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was pretty ambivalent about this until I read the transcript. Have an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very nice execution. The one thing you're really missing is a description of what the announcement looks like. Is there a test card on the screen? Or some other visuals? Is there an announcer? If so, what does he/she look and sound like? Can he/she be identified? Even better, put up a still of the announcement in progress.

Analogue televisions placed in SCP-1964's containment chamber can receive signals even when SCP-1964 cannot.

What? I think you mean something closer to "Other analogue televisions placed in SCP-1964's containment chamber function normally, and cannot receive the anomalous broadcasts."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Completely. I had a mental image of SCP-426 going on a rampage :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now I'm afraid to say I actually kinda like it- the addendum was creepy, in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Subjects will display symptoms of radiation illness including nausea, dizziness, vomiting, and fever when exposed by direct viewing. Subjects will develop radiation burns when continuously exposed. After a period ranging from a few days to several weeks following exposure subjects will display severe symptoms including internal hemorrhaging. Without treatment, subjects will die.

Cop out.

danger will robinson

Without the radiation it's just a TV that reads the scp wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't know how to feel. I don't hate it. I find the concept of an inanimate scp knowing its own number and claiming its own containment breach through the form of a television broadcast ve fairly interesting. What if although we contained the broadcast, its really a remnant of what we weren't around properly to contain long ago, and so it mocks us from some eldritch plane of existence?

Occam's razor, I know…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Non-standard format and it's just a thing what kills you while spouting random gibberish. I see where you were trying to go with the idea of signals from elsewhere in the multiverse, but it just rings hollow and I don't find it terrifying or interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I edited the next:

[EXPUNGED] for [DATA EXPUNGED].

Also with this sentence:

developed multiple cancers due to improper protection

I would recommend you this:

dveloped multiple cases of cancer due to improper protection

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's one thing that didn't sit well with me, there have been so many cases where 'oh, and it's radioactive and kills you' has been tacked on to make an item dangerous. Creepy and threatening is better received than outright dangerous with no defence ever time, I'd try another tack. It's still anomalous if it doesn't kill anyone, after all any CRT gives out radiation, so this pushes it into 'exaggerated effect' territory.

Also congratulations for not referencing other SCPs in an article where many would have done. Tentative +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not according to Wikipedia. It is ARS, Acute Radiation Syndrome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While you're at it TD, it's radiation sickness, not radiation syndrome. Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I could rewrite the addendum and add more.

Also, the radiation part I put there because if it was just a TV with classified info then it technically isn't anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Beyond the clichés, this doesn't do too many interesting things anyway. I almost started to get interested when the containment breach broadcast started, but it let me down that it was just one, and it kept repeating the same thing over and over. At least the description could have mentioned more examples of what was broadcasted so far… it still would be like the tome that describes alternate events when the Foundation messes things up and the world ends, but this one could describe them in news format and mention the events in detail.
And that radiation thing seems like an invidual addition that is there just to be there, or maybe to avoid "deletion because too few and cliché anomalies", it could at least be tied to the broadcasts in any way, like, the more grim the news are, the heavier the radiation…
Plus we already have a TV that knows about the Foundation in detail, it makes the show "Cash or Ash". And this TV is old AGAIN. Like most/kinda all other tv SCPs.

No vote so far, but as I write this comment, it starts to feel more and more as an intentional cliché collection…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I understand that some people will hate this on the cliche'd basis.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hated this until about halfway through the addendum, liked it a little, and the continued to hate it. My main issue with this is that it's another goddamn thing that kills you with radiation. I also didn't like how I predicted that this television broadcast would of course be about a nuclear crisis. While it was interesting that the Foundation was to blame for this prophesied catastrophe, it got a little too goofy as it went on.

If I were to like this at all, I feel that this were better off as a GoI object that they created to talk shit about the Foundation, rather than some ill omen from the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh… This isn't my favorite. Not sure what about it, but I just didn't care for it.

Good execution though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad, but I feel it could be better. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks :)

Sometimes it's hard to see the little things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
and is considered a Class-B Information Breach. For script, see Addenda 1964-1

to

and is considered a Class-B Information Breach. For script, see Addenda 1964-1.

Needed a period.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What was your edit?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. There's a few bits of awkward wording or iffy tone, but those can be cleared right up.

Question: am I the only one who thought of SCP-426 when it said "I have breached containment"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's just clarifying that they've tried putting similar TV's in the same place and seeing if they receive signals.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not totally original but very nicely executed I think. +1.

One quibble, though—

"Analogue televisions placed in SCP-1964's containment chamber can receive signals even when SCP-1964 cannot"

This needs more explanation. Is that implying that the scp aids them in receiving a signal, or that the scp is not really an analogue television, or that the scp sometimes can and sometimes can't pick up analogue signals?
